texts.full_text: arol Myers-Scotton (born 1934) is an American linguist. She was a Distinguished Professor Emerita in the Linguistics Program and Department of English at the University of South Carolina until 2003. Education She received her A.B. from Grinnell College in 1955, and her M.A. in English in 1961 and Ph.D. in linguistics in 1967, both from the University of Wisconsin–Madison. Career She resided in Columbia, South Carolina until 2003, where she was Carolina Distinguished Professor at the University of South Carolina in the Linguistics Program and Department of English. She currently resides in Michigan, where she is an adjunct professor in the Department of Linguistics and Languages at Michigan State University, and also a visiting scholar at the MSU African StudiCenter. Notable Work Myers-Scotton's work includes her code-switching theory known as "Markedness Model," which argues that speakers switch languages based on factors rather than linguistic ones. Her book titled Motivations for Code-Switching'" (1983) and '"Language Choice: Codes Switching in Bilinguals"'(2006), both published by Oxford University Press, are considered seminal works in the field of linguistics. Awards Myers-Scotton has received numerous awards throughout her career including: Guggenheim Fellowship for Natural Sciences and Mathematics/Physics/Paleontology/Chemistry/Biology/Linguistics/Mathematics/Astronomy/(Neuroscience) category in 2004 * Distinguished Service to the Profession of Linguistics award from The American Association for Applied Linguistics (AAAL) Legacy Carol Myers-Scotton's work has significantly contributed to our
